User: Find the least positive integer $n$ such that $$\frac 1{\sin 45^\circ\sin 46^\circ}+\frac 1{\sin 47^\circ\sin 48^\circ}+\cdots+\frac 1{\sin 133^\circ\sin 134^\circ}=\frac 1{\sin n^\circ}.$$
Assistant: <think>
response: Given that \( \frac{1}{\sin k^\circ \sin (k+1)^\circ} = \frac{1}{\sin 1^\circ} \left( \frac{\sin (k+1-k)^\circ}{\sin k^\circ \sin (k+1)^\circ} \right ) = \frac{1}{\sin 1^\circ} \left( \frac{\sin 1^\circ}{\sin k^\circ \sin (k+1)^\circ} \right ) = \frac{1}{\sin 1^\circ} \left( \frac{\cos k^\circ}{\sin k^\circ} - \frac{\cos (k+1)^\circ}{\sin (k+1)^\circ} \right ) = \frac{1}{\sin 1^\circ} \left( \cot k^\circ - \cot (k+1)^\circ \right ), \text{ a telescoping series arises when we sum from } k=45 \text{ to } k=133. \text{ Therefore, } \sum_{k=45}^{133} \frac{1}{\sin k^\circ \sin (k+1)^\circ} = \frac{1}{\sin 1^\circ} \left ( \cot 45^\circ - \cot 134^\circ \right ) = \frac{1}{\sin 1^\circ} \left ( 1 + \cot 46^\circ \right ). \text{ Since } \cot 46^\circ = \tan 44^\circ, \text{ we have } 1 + \tan 44^\circ = \frac{1}{\sin n^\circ}. \text{ We now need to find the angle } n \text{ such that } \sin n^\circ = \frac{1}{1 + \tan 44^\circ}. \text{ Using the identity } \tan 44^\circ = \frac{1 - \cos 88^\circ}{\sin 88^\circ}, \text{ and noting that } \cos 88^\circ = \sin 2^\circ \text{ and } \sin 88^\circ = \cos 2^\circ, \text{ we get } \tan 44^\circ = \frac{1 - \sin 2^\circ}{\cos 2^\circ}. \text{ Simplifying further, } 1 + \tan 44^\circ = 1 + \frac{1 - \sin 2^\circ}{\cos 2^\circ} = \frac{\cos 2^\circ + 1 - \sin 2^\circ}{\cos 2^\circ}. \text{ So, we need to find } \sin n^\circ = \frac{\cos 2^\circ}{\cos 2^\circ + 1 - \sin 2^\circ}. \text{ Note that } \frac{\cos 2^\circ}{\cos 2^\circ + 1 - \sin 2^\circ} = \sin 2^\circ. \text{ Therefore, } n = 2. </think> <answer> 2 </answer>

46^\circ) = -\cot 46^\circ, \text{ the expression simplifies to } \frac{1}{\sin 1^\circ} (1 + \cot 46^\circ). \text{ So the equation } \frac{1}{\sin n^\circ} = \frac{1}{\sin 1^\circ} (1 + \cot 46^\circ) \text{ implies } \sin n^\circ = \sin 1^\circ \left( 1 + \cot 46^\circ \right) = \sin 1^\circ \left( 1 + \frac{\cos 46^\circ}{\sin 46^\circ} \right) = \sin 1^\circ \left( \frac{\sin 46^\circ + \cos 46^\circ}{\sin 46^\circ} \right) = \frac{\sin 1^\circ \cos 46^\circ + \sin 1^\circ \sin 46^\circ}{\sin 46^\circ} = \frac{\sin (1^\circ + 46^\circ)}{\sin 46^\circ} = \frac{\sin 47^\circ}{\sin 46^\circ}. \text{ Finally, we need to find the angle whose sine is } \frac{\sin 47^\circ}{\sin 46^\circ}. \text{ Since } \frac{\sin 47^\circ}{\sin 46^\circ} = \sin 47^\circ \csc 46^\circ \text{ and } \csc 46^\circ \approx 1.035 \text{ (which is very close to 1), the angle whose sine is closest to } \sin 47^\circ \text{ is } 47^\circ. \text{ Therefore, the least positive integer } n \text{ is } 2. </think> <answer> 2 </answer>
